script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Tuesday — here is your QQQM recap. The QQQ portfolio closed zero point seven six percent down on the session. Semiconductor stocks took the hardest hit across the board, dragging technology broadly lower while a handful of mega-cap names managed to hold their ground. Here is what moved the portfolio.

Samsung Electronics reported a record quarter — and the market punished the entire semiconductor sector for it. That paradox is the defining story of Tuesday's session, and it ran straight through some of the heaviest weights in this portfolio.

The Nasdaq led all major indexes lower on Tuesday, as semiconductor stocks sold off sharply in response to Samsung's preliminary earnings report and rising crude oil prices added pressure to sentiment. The technology sector bore the brunt of the damage while communication services names actually bucked the trend, with select mega-caps posting meaningful gains. No major macroeconomic data prints were scheduled for Tuesday, leaving earnings and geopolitical headlines as the primary drivers of price action across the session.

Samsung Electronics reported operating profit roughly nineteen times higher than a year ago, with revenue more than doubling to a record figure — and yet the stock fell approximately seven percent. That outcome reverberated across the global memory and semiconductor supply chain on Tuesday. The read-through for Micron Technology was direct and damaging. If Samsung, the world's largest memory chipmaker, is delivering record output, supply concerns immediately surface. The market's logic: record Samsung production implies rising supply in DRAM and NAND, which pressures pricing, which compresses Micron's margins. Micron fell three dollars and eighty-one percent on the session, closing at nine hundred thirty-eight dollars and thirty-eight cents — a loss of thirty-seven dollars and eighteen cents. Separately, Yahoo Finance's reporting noted that Micron and Lam Research were named explicitly as session losers in broad market coverage, validating that the Samsung earnings read-through was the central catalyst. For QQQM holders, Micron sits at five point seven five percent of the portfolio — the fourth-largest weight — which means Tuesday's damage was not peripheral. This is a name to watch closely as SK Hynix's IPO approaches on Friday, which could introduce additional volatility into the memory complex.

Turning now to the full holdings rundown, beginning with the largest positions.

Nvidia closed at one hundred ninety-six dollars and ninety-three cents, up one point zero eight percent on Tuesday's session. That gain arrived despite a headline that deserves attention: DeepSeek, the Chinese artificial intelligence lab, is reportedly developing a custom AI chip specifically designed to reduce its reliance on Nvidia and to navigate United States export restrictions. Yahoo Finance's technology reporter covered the story, framing it as a deliberate effort by DeepSeek to build around the supply constraints that American trade policy has imposed. This is not a new theme — export controls have been a recurring overhang for Nvidia — but each incremental step toward homegrown Chinese AI silicon is a data point worth tracking. For now, Nvidia's session gain suggests the market treated the news as manageable rather than material. At seven point six percent of the portfolio, Nvidia remains the single largest weight, and any sustained development on the DeepSeek chip front would warrant reassessment.

Apple closed at three hundred ten dollars and sixty-six cents, up zero point six six percent on Tuesday's session. Two pieces of adjacent news circulated around Apple on Tuesday. First, Luxshare — a key Apple supplier — priced shares at the top of its range in Hong Kong's biggest initial public offering of the year, raising three point one billion dollars. Strong demand for an Apple supplier's equity offering speaks to continued confidence in the underlying hardware build-out. Second, a broader market story noted that Samsung's operating profit last quarter exceeded both Nvidia's and Apple's — a notable benchmark, even if it did not alter Apple's fundamental trajectory. Apple's session gain, while modest, held the line in a difficult tape. At six point eight percent of the portfolio, Apple is the second-largest position.

Over at Alphabet, the parent company of Google, shares closed at three hundred sixty-seven dollars and three cents, up one point nine eight percent on Tuesday's session. Alphabet was a standout performer in a losing session for the Nasdaq. On the news front, Leeward Renewable Energy celebrated milestones across a seven hundred twenty-five megawatt Oklahoma solar portfolio that directly supports Google operations, representing approximately one point five billion dollars in investment — a reminder of the scale of Alphabet's energy infrastructure commitments tied to its data center and artificial intelligence buildout. Separately, Google co-founder Sergey Brin accepted a ninety-four percent loss exiting a Manhattan real estate fund, a headline that generated noise but carries no operational relevance to Alphabet's business. The nearly two percent gain on Tuesday was a clean outperformer at six point one eight percent of the portfolio.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-eight dollars and eighty-four cents, down zero point four two percent on Tuesday's session. No company-specific news drove the move. The modest decline tracked the broader technology sector weakness and was contained relative to the damage seen elsewhere in the semiconductor names. At four point five two percent of the portfolio, Microsoft's relative calm in Tuesday's session was a stabilizing factor.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-five dollars and ninety-eight cents, up one point three six percent on Tuesday's session. No company-specific news was available for Tuesday. Amazon's gain ran counter to the broader Nasdaq weakness, consistent with consumer discretionary and cloud infrastructure names holding up better than pure semiconductor plays. At four point zero eight percent of the portfolio, the session performance was a mild positive.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred sixteen dollars and eleven cents, down zero point three three percent on Tuesday's session. The move was minimal and mirrored the modest pullback in semiconductor names that avoided the sharper Samsung-related selling. No company-specific news was available. At three point eight three percent of the portfolio, Advanced Micro Devices tracked the sector without amplifying it.

Tesla closed at four hundred two dollars and ninety cents, up two point four zero percent on Tuesday's session — a gain of nine dollars and forty-five cents. No company-specific news drove the move, but the gain made Tesla one of the better performers among primary holdings on a difficult day for the Nasdaq. Consumer discretionary names with less direct semiconductor exposure had room to run while chip stocks absorbed the selling pressure. At three point zero nine percent of the portfolio, Tuesday's move was a clean contributor.

Intel closed at one hundred ten dollars and thirty-nine cents, down eight point two eight percent on Tuesday's session — a loss of nine dollars and ninety-six cents. That is one of the steepest single-session declines across the entire primary holdings list on Tuesday. No isolated Intel-specific headline drove the magnitude of the drop, but the Samsung earnings read-through hit legacy semiconductor names hard, and Intel sits squarely in that category. At two point nine zero percent of the portfolio, an eight-plus percent single-day loss is material and warrants close attention as the semiconductor sector works through the Samsung supply narrative.

On the secondary holdings front, two names demand mention. Meta Platforms closed at six hundred fifteen dollars and fifty-eight cents, up five point six one percent on Tuesday's session — a gain of thirty-two dollars and sixty-eight cents. That is the single largest percentage gain across all tracked holdings on Tuesday, and it acted as a meaningful counterweight to the semiconductor damage at its two point six six percent portfolio weight. Applied Materials closed at five hundred fifty-four dollars and fifty cents, down eight point zero five percent — a loss of forty-eight dollars and fifty-four cents — directly tied to the Samsung-driven semiconductor selloff. Lam Research and KLA Corporation also fell sharply, down seven point one nine percent and eight point one zero percent respectively, deepening the semiconductor sector damage across secondary names.

Three numbers to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, SK Hynix's initial public offering, expected Friday — as a direct competitor to Micron in the DRAM market, pricing and early trading will signal how investors are digesting the Samsung supply narrative, which hit Micron hard on Tuesday. Second, any updated analyst commentary on Nvidia's export exposure following the DeepSeek chip development report — Nvidia is the portfolio's largest single weight at seven point six percent, and incremental China supply chain news is an active risk. Third, watch for any Federal Reserve speaker appearances mid-week — no Fed commentary was available for Tuesday, but rate trajectory remains the structural backdrop for technology valuations across the entire portfolio.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Keep close watch on the memory sector — the Samsung ripple is still moving through the supply chain, and Friday's SK Hynix event could set the tone for the back half of the week.
